title: Research
text:
I investigate whether renewable energy penetration shields US counties from higher energy prices stemming from exogenous oil price shocks, using the Russia-Ukraine War as a natural experiment.






Do autocratic-mandated leaders erode democratic institutions, and through what channels? Are these systems capable of preventing backsliding? To answer these questions, I construct a new database of autocratic executives (e.g. Prime Ministers, Presidents) across over 50 competitive democracies from 1945 to 2025 using an ex-ante definition based on campaign manifestos. First, I show that the election of an autocratic-mandated leader strongly predicts whether a country eventually transitions into autocracy. Second, I show using staggered difference-in-differences methods that, once in power, these leaders gradually weaken democratic institutions and judicial constraints on the executive. They do so by conducting judicial purges, court packing, non-compliance. Third, I show that their inaugurations are followed by weakened media independence, increased government media censorship, and a rise in journalist harassment. These results show that these leaders are constrained by existing institutional guardrails but these constraints are eroded overtime.

title: Research
text:
Theses

A Crisis of Consensus: Austerity and the Rise of Political Polarization
MRes thesis, Sciences Po Paris, May 2025. Supervisor: Dr. Moritz Schularick. Jury: Dr. Kevin O’Rourke. Grade: 16/20 (A).
This study examines the political consequences of fiscal consolidations using a comprehensive dataset of austerity shocks and electoral outcomes across 15 advanced democracies from 1978 to 2016. It finds that austerity shocks significantly increase political polarization and support for non-mainstream parties over a four-year horizon, driven by austerity-induced economic insecurity. These results remain robust when controlling for financial crises and endogeneity. While the economic costs of austerity are larger during slumps than booms, the political effects are unexpectedly more pronounced when austerity is implemented during economic expansions. This divergence from the predictions of the economic insecurity channel suggests the presence of state-heterogeneous political responses to fiscal consolidation, offering new directions for future research.

title: Eli Cheam Shapiro
text:
Eli Cheam Shapiro
    Analyst, Penn Wharton Budget Model
    MRes in Economics · Sciences Po Paris

    
      In June 2026, I joined the
      Penn Wharton Budget Model
      at the University of Pennsylvania as an analyst. My research is on public finance, inequality, political economy, and energy economics, with a special focus on the distributional impacts of fiscal policy and public investment.
    

    
      Previously, I was a predoctoral research assistant at Trinity College Dublin.
      I hold an MRes in Economics from Sciences Po Paris
      and a BA in Economics from Bard College.
    

    
      I am proficient in R, Python, STATA, and Julia.
    

    
      
         elicheam@wharton.upenn.edu
